title: Federal Communications Commission v. AT&T, Inc.
backgroundAndFacts:
The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) issued regulations affecting how telecommunications carriers like AT&T and Verizon operate. AT&T and Verizon challenged these regulations in the United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit, arguing that the FCC exceeded its statutory authority. The Fifth Circuit, in a decision issued August 22, 2025, ruled in favor of AT&T and Verizon, striking down or limiting the FCC's regulations. The FCC then petitioned the Supreme Court for review, and the case was consolidated with a related case (No. 25-567) for briefing and oral argument.

This consolidated case presents fundamental questions about the proper scope of regulatory authority granted to federal agencies under telecommunications law. The lower court's decision appears to have restricted how the FCC can implement its statutory mandate, prompting the government to seek Supreme Court review of that limitation.
legalQuestion: What is the scope of the FCC's authority to regulate telecommunications carriers, and did the Fifth Circuit correctly limit the agency's regulatory power?
significance:
This case could significantly impact telecommunications regulation and the balance of power between federal agencies and the courts. If the Supreme Court affirms the Fifth Circuit's narrow interpretation of FCC authority, it may limit the agency's ability to adapt its regulations to new technologies and market conditions. Conversely, if the Court reverses, it would reaffirm broader FCC authority and potentially signal support for flexible agency regulation in other industries. The decision will affect not only how AT&T, Verizon, and other carriers operate, but also the broader question of how much deference courts should give to agency interpretations of their own statutory powers.

The consolidation of this case with another related case (No. 25-567) suggests that multiple carriers and perhaps other stakeholders challenged overlapping FCC regulations, making this a high-stakes dispute with industry-wide implications.

parties.party: Federal Communications Commission and United States
parties.coreArgument: The FCC possessed statutory authority to issue the challenged regulations, and the Fifth Circuit improperly restricted the agency's delegated power. The Court should reverse and recognize that the FCC's interpretation of its regulatory mandate is entitled to deference as a reasonable exercise of agency discretion.
supportingPoints: The FCC's statutory authority under telecommunications law encompasses the regulatory power exercised
Agency interpretations of ambiguous statutes merit judicial deference under established administrative law principles
The Fifth Circuit's decision creates regulatory uncertainty and limits the FCC's ability to govern the telecommunications industry effectively
The lower court's narrow reading of FCC authority is inconsistent with the scope of powers delegated by Congress

parties.party: AT&T, Inc. and Verizon Communications Inc.
parties.coreArgument: The FCC regulations at issue exceed the agency's statutory authority and violate the Administrative Procedure Act. The Fifth Circuit correctly identified the limits of the FCC's delegated power, and the Supreme Court should affirm, holding that regulatory authority must remain within the boundaries Congress established.
supportingPoints: The FCC exceeded its statutory mandate by regulating conduct or entities outside its delegated scope
The regulations were arbitrary and capricious or violated procedural requirements under the Administrative Procedure Act
Carriers are entitled to clear statutory authorization before bearing compliance burdens imposed by agency regulation
The Fifth Circuit's decision properly cabins agency power within constitutionally sound limits on delegation

citedPrecedents.caseName: Chevron U.S.A., Inc. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc.
citedPrecedents.reasonCited: This seminal case establishes the standard of judicial review for agency interpretations of statutes they administer, which is central to whether the FCC's interpretation of its regulatory authority is entitled to deference.
This recent decision overruled Chevron deference and fundamentally changed how courts review agency interpretations, likely affecting the standard applicable to the FCC's regulatory authority claims.

This foundational statute grants the FCC its regulatory authority over telecommunications carriers and services, making its scope a central issue in determining whether the FCC's regulations were authorized.

The APA establishes procedural requirements for agency rulemaking and provides the standard for 'arbitrary and capricious' review, which AT&T and Verizon likely invoked in challenging the FCC's actions.
This case refines the scope of Chevron deference by requiring that an agency interpretation receive deference only if the agency was delegated authority to make rules with the force of law.

citedPrecedents.caseName: Brand X Internet Services v. Federal Communications Commission
citedPrecedents.reasonCited: This case involved FCC regulatory authority over broadband internet services and the scope of the agency's power under the Communications Act, presenting similar jurisdictional questions.
